title: Lemonade
subtitle: Fresh lemon juice and sugar syrup topped up with sparkling water.
excerpt: Fresh lemon juice stirred into a simple sugar syrup, then diluted with sparkling water and poured over ice. Tastes completely different from bottled lemonade. Takes fifteen minutes.
sourceNotes: Lemonade in the British sense, a clear sparkling drink made from lemon juice and sugar, has been a summer staple since at least the Victorian era, when lemon juice and soda water were sold from street stalls. The homemade version using a cooked sugar syrup to suspend the lemon flavour produces a cleaner, more stable drink than simply stirring sugar into juice.

content.text: The sugar syrup step is not optional. Cold water does not dissolve sugar properly: you get a gritty, sweet drink with uneven flavour. A quick cooked syrup takes five minutes and lasts two weeks in the fridge, so you can make lemonade to order from it.
Use the lemon zest as well as the juice. The zest contains the aromatic oils that give lemon its fragrance; juice alone produces a sharp but flat drink. Add the zest to the syrup while it cooks.
A small saucepan, a measuring jug, and a fine sieve.

Put {{caster-sugar}} of sugar and {{water}} of water in a small saucepan with the lemon zest. Heat over a medium heat, stirring, until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a brief simmer for 1 minute, then remove from the heat. Leave to cool for 10 minutes. Strain through a sieve into a jug, pressing on the zest to extract the oils. Stir in the lemon juice.

Fill glasses with ice. Pour the lemon syrup over the ice, then top up with {{sparkling-water}} of chilled sparkling water. Stir and taste: add more syrup or sparkling water to adjust sweetness and strength. Garnish with mint if using.

items.fix: Add more sparkling water. The ratio of syrup to sparkling water is flexible, adjust to taste.
items.cause: Too much syrup relative to sparkling water.

items.fix: Cool the syrup fully before diluting. Add the sparkling water cold, and stir gently rather than vigorously.
items.cause: Sparkling water added while syrup was still warm, releasing the bubbles.

items.fix: Zest is where the intense flavour lives. Add more zest to the syrup next time, or use the full zest quantity.
items.cause: Not enough zest in the syrup, or too little syrup relative to water.

items.fix: Warm briefly and stir to dissolve. This happens if the syrup was not stirred enough while cooling.
items.cause: The sugar crystallised as it cooled.

content.text: Homemade lemonade is a summer ritual in Britain. It sits alongside Wimbledon, summer fêtes, and back-garden cricket. Most shop versions are made from lemon flavouring, fizz, and sweetener rather than real lemon. That is why homemade tastes so different from the bottled kind: sharper and less sweet. This version uses a simple sugar syrup and fresh lemon. The ratio is easy to adjust to taste.
